name: Brick Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Brick. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for brick surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal brick absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution on brick surface texture

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal while preserving brick substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage and process efficiency

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal from porous brick surface

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal accumulation

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm Nd:YAG lasers in stone/brick cleaning to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max avoids cracking due to brick's low thermal conductivity (~0.8 W/mK). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics (e.g., YLR series for restoration); academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Stone Surfaces' (Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2015).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contaminant removal on porous brick; slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy soiling like soot, faster (500-2000 mm/s) for light dust. Recommended 500 mm/s optimizes cleaning rate while minimizing heat buildup. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines; case studies in Journal of Cultural Heritage (2018) on brick restoration.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) preferred for brick to ablate contaminants via photothermal effects with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduces HAZ further but increases cost. Recommended 20 ns for balance. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2017) on stone ablation thresholds; avoids fs due to brick's high damage risk from shockwaves.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning to control energy delivery; 10-50 kHz for brick prevents heat accumulation in low-conductivity material, while higher rates speed up process but risk thermal stress. Recommended 30 kHz for optimal throughput. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2014) on pulse repetition effects in cultural heritage cleaning; trade-off noted in manufacturer manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe ablation of organic/inorganic contaminants on brick; damage threshold ~3-5 J/cm² to avoid surface cracking or vitrification. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for effective cleaning below threshold. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2016) on fired clay materials; conservative for brick's porosity and reflectivity (~20-30% at 1064 nm).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; smaller (0.5-1 mm) for detailed work, larger (2-5 mm) for broad brick surfaces. Recommended 2 mm for uniform coverage on rough brick texture. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for surface preparation; optimized in process studies (Lasers in Engineering, 2019).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers to ensure complete removal without excessive energy. Recommended 2 passes for brick to achieve uniform results while staying below damage threshold. Source: Industry practice in restoration case studies (e.g., European Commission reports on laser cleaning of masonry, 2020); avoids overexposure on porous surfaces.

overlap_ratio.description: Overlap ensures uniform coverage on uneven brick; 30-50% for speed, 60-80% for thorough cleaning of pores. Recommended 60% for optimal uniformity without redundancy. Source: Process optimization studies in Applied Optics (2015) on scan strategies for stone; standard in manufacturer guidelines (e.g., Laserax equipment manuals).
